catalyst.source: The obtained Ag quantum dots modified TiO 2 (AgTiO 2 ) shows excellent and stable photocatalytic activity in CO 2 reduction.
catalyst.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into high added-value chemical products has received special attention.Herein, we designed a novel hierarchically porous and defective TiO 2 modified by Ag quantum dots via a hydrothermal process assisted with organic surfactant and subsequently in-situ photodeposition method.The obtained Ag quantum dots modified TiO 2 (AgTiO 2 ) shows excellent and stable photocatalytic activity in CO 2 reduction.In addition, the competing H 2 evolution can be completely suppressed.The super photocatalytic activity can be ascribed to the design of hierarchically porous, the intrinsic defects in TiO 2 , the localized surface plasmon resonance of Ag and the synergetic effect between Ag quantum dots and defective TiO 2 , which jointly improves the CO 2 adsorption performance, enhances visible light absorption capacity and accelerates the separation of photogenerated charges.This work may provide a strategy to design and synthesis defective and hierarchically porous metal oxides for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction.

catalyst.source: After being filtered and washed by water solution, the precipitate was collected after drying at 100 C for 8 h and the final sample was denoted as AgTiO 2 .
catalyst.context: Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, AR, 25 wt) was purchased from Aladdin, China.Titanium sulfate (Ti(SO 4 ) 2 , CR), commercial TiO 2 (CR), reference P25 (CR) and silver nitrate (AgNO 3 , CR) were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd without further purification in this work. Typically, 7.2 g Ti(SO 4 ) 2 were mixed with 30 mL H 2 O and 5 g TPAOH and vigorously stirred at 40 C for 4 h.Next, the resulting solution was hydrothermal treated at 150 C for 16 h.After being cooled to room temperature, the solution was washed three times with distilled water and dried 100 C for 8 h.Finally, the light-gray powder was collected after calcination at 550 C for 4 h. 0.1 mL AgNO 3 aqueous solution (0.2 M) was added into the 100 mL TiO 2 suspension aqueous solution (0.5 g) and stirred for 2 h at room temperature, and then the solution was irradiated for 30 min under Xe light to form Ag quantum dots.After being filtered and washed by water solution, the precipitate was collected after drying at 100 C for 8 h and the final sample was denoted as AgTiO 2 . The powder P25 was calcined at 550 C in H 2 Ar for 4 h and lightyellow defective P25 has been obtained. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Rigaku DMax 2200PC diffractometer with Cu K radiation (40 kV and 40 mA).UV visible absorption spectrum (UV-vis) was investigated by the Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer, and BaSO 4 was selected as the reference sample.Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were recorded on the Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrometer using a 325 nm excitation wavelength.The nitrogen sorptiondesorption curves of the samples were tested on Micromeritics ASAP 3020, and the specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods at -196 C, respectively.The morphologies and the dispersion of Ag of the sample were observed by the Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM, JEOL-2010F) and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, FEI, Magellan 400) with a high angle angular dark field (HAADF) detector, and the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrometer was recorded on the TEM (JEOL-2010F).The temperature-programed desorption with CO 2 (CO 2 -TPD) curves were recorded on Micrometrics Chemisorb 2750 instrument attached with ChemiSoft TPx software.The content of Ag was obtained on the Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy analyzer (ICP-OES).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on the ESCAlab 250Xi instrument, and the binding energies were calibrated by the C 1s electron peak (BE 284.8eV).Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on the Nicolet 7000-C instrument.The carbon isotope analysis was carried out on the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS, Agilent Technologies 7890A-5795C). The photoelectrochemical tests were conducted in Na 2 SO 4 (0.5 M) solution on the CHI 660A electrochemical workstation with three electrode system.A 300 W Xe lamp was employed as light source.A Pt sheet and AgAgCl electrode were used as opposite and reference electrode, respectively.In the preparing process of work electrode, 40 mg of catalyst was dispersed into 1 mL ethanol by ultrasonic technique to form a slurry.Next, 0.3 mL slurry was coated on carbon paper with an area of 2 cm 2 and dried at 100 C for 4 h. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was measured in an 80 mL gasclosed system under UV-vis light irradiation emitted from a 300 W Xe lamp without any light filter which positioned 10 cm above the reactor.A suspension of catalyst (50 mg) and deionized water (40 mL) was added into the reactor with vigorous stirring.The photoreaction system was purged with the high-purity CO 2 gas for 30 min before the lamp tune on, and the temperature of reactor was controlled at 20 C via using cooling water circulation.The reduction products were collected at given intervals (1 h) and analyzed by a gas chromatography equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID).

catalyst.source: The defective TiO 2 nanoparticles with hierarchically porous structure have been successfully prepared by a hydrothermal process assisted with organic surfactant, and Ag quantum dot as a cocatalyst have been uniformly stabilized on the surface of TiO 2 nanoparticles via in-situ photodeposition methods.
catalyst.context: The defective TiO 2 nanoparticles with hierarchically porous structure have been successfully prepared by a hydrothermal process assisted with organic surfactant, and Ag quantum dot as a cocatalyst have been uniformly stabilized on the surface of TiO 2 nanoparticles via in-situ photodeposition methods.As anticipated, the obtained samples TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 exhibit excellent and stable photocatalytic CO 2 reduction performance compared to commercial TiO 2 .The super photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the design of hierarchically porous structure, the creature of defects and the modification of Ag quantum dots, which can significantly improve the CO 2 adsorption capacity and visible light absorption performance, and greatly accelerate the separation of photogenerated charges.Additionally, the synergetic effect between Ag and TiO 2 can further promote CO 2 adsorption and the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs via the Schootty barrier, which makes it own enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction activity. Fig. 1.The XRD patterns of the samples commercial TiO 2 and the prepared hierarchically porous TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 . Fig. 3. a) Low-magnification scanning electron micrograph (SEM) and b-c) Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) images, (d-f) the high-magnification TEM images for the sample AgTiO 2 . Fig. 4. The BF image and the corresponding elemental signals of the hierarchically porous AgTiO 2 sample. Fig. 6. a) UV-vis adsorption spectra, b) PL spectra and c) Transient photocurrent densities of the commercial TiO 2 and the prepared hierarchically porous TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 . Fig. 7. a) The temperature programmed desorption of CO 2 (CO 2 -TPD) and b) CO yield of the commercial TiO 2 and the prepared hierarchically porous TiO 2 and Ag TiO 2 with different time under UV-vis light irradiation, c) the cycle stability of the sample AgTiO 2 in the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction and d) the schematic illustration of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction on the sample AgTiO 2 under UV-vis light irradiation. This research was sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51502330, 21665007, 21964007), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (19ZR1464500) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201801D211011). The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https:doi.org10.1016j.cej.2020.128397.

Fig. 3. a) Low-magnification scanning electron micrograph (SEM) and b-c) Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) images, (d-f) the high-magnification TEM images for the sample AgTiO 2 . Fig. 4. Fig. 6. a) UV-vis adsorption spectra, b) PL spectra and c) Transient photocurrent densities of the commercial TiO 2 and the prepared hierarchically porous TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 . Fig. 7. a) The temperature programmed desorption of CO 2 (CO 2 -TPD) and b) CO yield of the commercial TiO 2 and the prepared hierarchically porous TiO 2 and Ag TiO 2 with different time under UV-vis light irradiation, c) the cycle stability of the sample AgTiO 2 in the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction and d) the schematic illustration of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction on the sample AgTiO 2 under UV-vis light irradiation. co-catalyst.source: Herein, we designed a novel hierarchically porous and defective TiO 2 modified by Ag quantum dots via a hydrothermal process assisted with organic surfactant and subsequently in-situ photodeposition method.
co-catalyst.context: Photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into high added-value chemical products has received special attention.Herein, we designed a novel hierarchically porous and defective TiO 2 modified by Ag quantum dots via a hydrothermal process assisted with organic surfactant and subsequently in-situ photodeposition method.The obtained Ag quantum dots modified TiO 2 (AgTiO 2 ) shows excellent and stable photocatalytic activity in CO 2 reduction.In addition, the competing H 2 evolution can be completely suppressed.The super photocatalytic activity can be ascribed to the design of hierarchically porous, the intrinsic defects in TiO 2 , the localized surface plasmon resonance of Ag and the synergetic effect between Ag quantum dots and defective TiO 2 , which jointly improves the CO 2 adsorption performance, enhances visible light absorption capacity and accelerates the separation of photogenerated charges.This work may provide a strategy to design and synthesis defective and hierarchically porous metal oxides for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction.

co-catalyst.source: 0.1 mL AgNO 3 aqueous solution (0.2 M) was added into the 100 mL TiO 2 suspension aqueous solution (0.5 g) and stirred for 2 h at room temperature, and then the solution was irradiated for 30 min under Xe light to form Ag quantum dots.
co-catalyst.context: Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, AR, 25 wt) was purchased from Aladdin, China.Titanium sulfate (Ti(SO 4 ) 2 , CR), commercial TiO 2 (CR), reference P25 (CR) and silver nitrate (AgNO 3 , CR) were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd without further purification in this work. Typically, 7.2 g Ti(SO 4 ) 2 were mixed with 30 mL H 2 O and 5 g TPAOH and vigorously stirred at 40 C for 4 h.Next, the resulting solution was hydrothermal treated at 150 C for 16 h.After being cooled to room temperature, the solution was washed three times with distilled water and dried 100 C for 8 h.Finally, the light-gray powder was collected after calcination at 550 C for 4 h. 0.1 mL AgNO 3 aqueous solution (0.2 M) was added into the 100 mL TiO 2 suspension aqueous solution (0.5 g) and stirred for 2 h at room temperature, and then the solution was irradiated for 30 min under Xe light to form Ag quantum dots.After being filtered and washed by water solution, the precipitate was collected after drying at 100 C for 8 h and the final sample was denoted as AgTiO 2 . The powder P25 was calcined at 550 C in H 2 Ar for 4 h and lightyellow defective P25 has been obtained. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Rigaku DMax 2200PC diffractometer with Cu K radiation (40 kV and 40 mA).UV visible absorption spectrum (UV-vis) was investigated by the Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer, and BaSO 4 was selected as the reference sample.Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were recorded on the Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrometer using a 325 nm excitation wavelength.The nitrogen sorptiondesorption curves of the samples were tested on Micromeritics ASAP 3020, and the specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods at -196 C, respectively.The morphologies and the dispersion of Ag of the sample were observed by the Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM, JEOL-2010F) and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, FEI, Magellan 400) with a high angle angular dark field (HAADF) detector, and the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrometer was recorded on the TEM (JEOL-2010F).The temperature-programed desorption with CO 2 (CO 2 -TPD) curves were recorded on Micrometrics Chemisorb 2750 instrument attached with ChemiSoft TPx software.The content of Ag was obtained on the Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy analyzer (ICP-OES).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on the ESCAlab 250Xi instrument, and the binding energies were calibrated by the C 1s electron peak (BE 284.8eV).Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on the Nicolet 7000-C instrument.The carbon isotope analysis was carried out on the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS, Agilent Technologies 7890A-5795C). The photoelectrochemical tests were conducted in Na 2 SO 4 (0.5 M) solution on the CHI 660A electrochemical workstation with three electrode system.A 300 W Xe lamp was employed as light source.A Pt sheet and AgAgCl electrode were used as opposite and reference electrode, respectively.In the preparing process of work electrode, 40 mg of catalyst was dispersed into 1 mL ethanol by ultrasonic technique to form a slurry.Next, 0.3 mL slurry was coated on carbon paper with an area of 2 cm 2 and dried at 100 C for 4 h. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was measured in an 80 mL gasclosed system under UV-vis light irradiation emitted from a 300 W Xe lamp without any light filter which positioned 10 cm above the reactor.A suspension of catalyst (50 mg) and deionized water (40 mL) was added into the reactor with vigorous stirring.The photoreaction system was purged with the high-purity CO 2 gas for 30 min before the lamp tune on, and the temperature of reactor was controlled at 20 C via using cooling water circulation.The reduction products were collected at given intervals (1 h) and analyzed by a gas chromatography equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID).

Typically, 7.2 g Ti(SO 4 ) 2 were mixed with 30 mL H 2 O and 5 g TPAOH and vigorously stirred at 40 C for 4 h.Next, the resulting solution was hydrothermal treated at 150 C for 16 h.After being cooled to room temperature, the solution was washed three times with distilled water and dried 100 C for 8 h.Finally, the light-gray powder was collected after calcination at 550 C for 4 h. 0.1 mL AgNO 3 aqueous solution (0.2 M) was added into the 100 mL TiO 2 suspension aqueous solution (0.5 g) and stirred for 2 h at room temperature, and then the solution was irradiated for 30 min under Xe light to form Ag quantum dots.After being filtered and washed by water solution, the precipitate was collected after drying at 100 C for 8 h and the final sample was denoted as AgTiO 2 . The powder P25 was calcined at 550 C in H 2 Ar for 4 h and lightyellow defective P25 has been obtained. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Rigaku DMax 2200PC diffractometer with Cu K radiation (40 kV and 40 mA).UV visible absorption spectrum (UV-vis) was investigated by the Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer, and BaSO 4 was selected as the reference sample.Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were recorded on the Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrometer using a 325 nm excitation wavelength.The nitrogen sorptiondesorption curves of the samples were tested on Micromeritics ASAP 3020, and the specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods at -196 C, respectively.The morphologies and the dispersion of Ag of the sample were observed by the Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM, JEOL-2010F) and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, FEI, Magellan 400) with a high angle angular dark field (HAADF) detector, and the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrometer was recorded on the TEM (JEOL-2010F).The temperature-programed desorption with CO 2 (CO 2 -TPD) curves were recorded on Micrometrics Chemisorb 2750 instrument attached with ChemiSoft TPx software.The content of Ag was obtained on the Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy analyzer (ICP-OES).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on the ESCAlab 250Xi instrument, and the binding energies were calibrated by the C 1s electron peak (BE 284.8eV).Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on the Nicolet 7000-C instrument.The carbon isotope analysis was carried out on the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS, Agilent Technologies 7890A-5795C). The photoelectrochemical tests were conducted in Na 2 SO 4 (0.5 M) solution on the CHI 660A electrochemical workstation with three electrode system.A 300 W Xe lamp was employed as light source.A Pt sheet and AgAgCl electrode were used as opposite and reference electrode, respectively.In the preparing process of work electrode, 40 mg of catalyst was dispersed into 1 mL ethanol by ultrasonic technique to form a slurry.Next, 0.3 mL slurry was coated on carbon paper with an area of 2 cm 2 and dried at 100 C for 4 h. The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was measured in an 80 mL gasclosed system under UV-vis light irradiation emitted from a 300 W Xe lamp without any light filter which positioned 10 cm above the reactor.A suspension of catalyst (50 mg) and deionized water (40 mL) was added into the reactor with vigorous stirring.The photoreaction system was purged with the high-purity CO 2 gas for 30 min before the lamp tune on, and the temperature of reactor was controlled at 20 C via using cooling water circulation.The reduction products were collected at given intervals (1 h) and analyzed by a gas chromatography equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID).
